subject: english
learning_objectives: Distinguish between definite and indefinite articles
Apply the rules for using 'a', 'an', and 'the' correctly
Identify special cases involving vowel and consonant sounds

sections.heading: Indefinite Articles: 'a' and 'an'
sections.content:
'A' and 'AN' are used to make indefinite reference to a noun.

Use 'A' before words beginning with a consonant SOUND:
a boy, a goat, a house, a hoe
NOTE: Some words begin with a vowel LETTER but have a consonant SOUND – use 'a':
a European man | a university | a Eucharist | a eunuch | a unit | a union | a uniform

Use 'AN' before words beginning with a vowel SOUND:
an egg, an apple, an orange
NOTE: Some words have a silent 'h' – use 'an':
an hour | an honest man | an honour
BUT words with a sounded 'h' take 'a': a house | a hoe | a home

sections.heading: Definite Article: 'the'
sections.content:
'The' makes definite reference to a specific person or thing. All nouns can use 'the'.

Uses of 'the':

1. When there is only one of the thing referred to:
the sun | the moon | the wind | the rainfall

2. When the hearer/reader knows which specific thing is meant:
Give me the bag under the bed.
The letter in the envelope. (Both objects are already known.)

3. Before the title of a newspaper:
The Punch | The Nation

4. Descriptive use – the article describes or particularises the noun:
The accused was acquitted for want of evidence.
She has the courage of Esther.

5. Generic use – referring to what is typical for a class:
The banana is found mainly in southern Nigeria.
The tiger is a very ferocious animal.

6. Before nouns indicating unique position or role:
The Soviet Union | The Principal | The Minister of Education

sections.heading: Summary Table
sections.content:
Noun type           | Indefinite reference | Definite reference
Singular count noun | A desk               | The desk
Plural count noun   | Some books           | The books
Non-count noun      | Some water / Water   | The water
